title: Floquet Entanglement Generation in Parametrically Driven Coupled Superconducting Qubits
authors: Gustavo M. Meneses A., Daniel Dominguez, María José Sánchez
abstract: We investigate the dynamical generation of entanglement in a system of two superconducting qubits coupled through a parametrically driven longitudinal interaction. Using Floquet theory and exact numerical simulations, we analyze the time evolution of the system initialized in a separable ground state. Our results reveal a nontrivial mechanism for entanglement generation, fundamentally distinct from the conventional resonant excitation to an entangled eigenstate. We show that this mechanism emerges when two initially separable eigenstates are mixed by the periodic driving under multiphoton resonance conditions. Since the effect cannot be captured within a standard rotating-wave approximation, we employ generalized Van Vleck near-degenerate perturbation theory to derive an effective analytical description. Within this framework, we demonstrate that the sustained entanglement originates from the hybridization of the dominant Floquet states, namely those with the largest overlap with the initial ground state. Furthermore, the degree of entanglement can be efficiently controlled through the driving amplitude. In particular, for specific amplitudes, the entanglement is fully suppressed. We term this phenomenon as coherent destruction of entanglement.
summary: This paper theoretically analyzes how parametric driving generates and controls entanglement in coupled superconducting qubits. It establishes that the generation mechanism relies on Floquet state hybridization under specific resonance conditions (SSR). The work also predicts a controllable suppression of this entanglement, termed Coherent Destruction of Entanglement.
main_problem: The study investigates how robust, sustained entanglement can be dynamically generated in a coupled superconducting qubit system using parametric driving fields.
main_result: A nontrivial mechanism for Floquet entanglement generation (FEG) is shown to occur under specific resonance conditions (SSR), which is distinct from conventional excitation methods. Furthermore, the phenomenon of Coherent Destruction of Entanglement (CDE) allows for controlled suppression of this generated entanglement.
method: The analysis employs Floquet theory combined with generalized Van Vleck near-degenerate perturbation theory to derive an effective Hamiltonian governing the system's dynamics.
model_or_system: The model consists of two superconducting qubits coupled via a time-periodic, parametrically driven longitudinal interaction. The system is analyzed using its time-dependent Hamiltonian and subsequent Floquet quasienergy spectrum.
key_observables: Double-averaged concurrence ($C$), which quantifies entanglement; transition probabilities ($P_{ij}$); dynamic Stark shifts ($\delta_{\uparrow\uparrow}/\delta_{\downarrow\downarrow}$) and effective coupling ($u$).
important_parameters: Driving amplitude ($A$) relative to $\omega$, detunings ($\epsilon_0/\omega, J_0/\omega$), and the resonance conditions (SSR vs. SER).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies heavily on second-order perturbation theory (GVV approximation) because standard approximations like RWA fail under certain resonance conditions.
figures_summary: Figures map out the time-averaged concurrence ($C$) in parameter space, highlighting strong entanglement patterns near resonances and demonstrating how $C$ is suppressed when the effective coupling vanishes (CDE).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical model and identifying resonance types (SER vs. SSR), to employing advanced perturbation theory (GVV) to derive an effective Hamiltonian, and finally analyzing the resulting entanglement dynamics, including the mechanism for its suppression (CDE).
why_interesting: This work provides a detailed theoretical framework for engineering quantum resources like entanglement in solid-state qubits using time-periodic driving, which is crucial for building scalable quantum processors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a)Energy spectrum of the static Hamiltonian H0 as function of the normalized static coupling J0/ω for ϵ0/ω = 1. The top-right inset shows a zoom of the quasi-degeneracy of E2 and E3, while the bottom-right inset highlights the avoided crossing at J0/ω = ϵ0/ω. (b) Color map of the ground state concurrence C, as a function of {ϵ0/ω, J0/ω}. Two entangled regions can be identified:(i) a triangular region for |J0/ω| < |ϵ0/ω| in which C0 ≈1 and (ii) a narrow band around ϵ0/ω ≈0. Outside these regions, the ground state is nearly separable.

Figure 2. (a) Color map of C (Eq.(7)), in the parameter space {ϵ0/ω, J0/ω} for A/ω = 3.8. Patterns where entanglement is strong emerge within the parameter regime that corresponds to nearly separable region (J0/ω > 0) in the static case (see Fig.1(b)). (b) C for two different resonance conditions. For ϵ0/ω = 1.11 localized entanglement peaks (C < 0.5) appear whenever the system reaches an SER condition. In contrast, for the n = 2 SSR condition (ϵ0/ω = 1) a broad and robust enhancement of entanglement is obtained with C ≈0.75 over a wide range of J0/ω. Time evolution was averaged over t/T = 700 and 64 distinct initial phases φ0.

Figure 3. Color map of C as a function of {J0/ω, A/ω}. (a) For ϵ0/ω = 1.11 resonances where C ≈.5 are observed only for values of J0/ω satisfying the SER condition. (b) For the SSR condition ϵ0/ω = 1 (nω = 2) the concurrence predominantly exceeds 0.8, exhibiting moderate modulations as a function of the driving amplitude A/ω. See text for more details.

Figure 4. Double-averaged transition probabilities Pij in the parameter space {J0/ω, A/ω} for ϵ0/ω = 1 (nω = 2) SSR con- dition. A clear change in the plots is observed at J0/ω = ϵ0/ω, corresponding to the location of the avoided in the spectrum of H0 (see Sec.II for details). (a) P01 depicts broad SSR reso- nances patterns for the region J0/ω > 1, where |ϕ1⟩is nearly separable. (b) P02 displays narrow, amplitude-modulated, fringes for values of J0/ω satisfying the SER condition. In this case |ϕ2⟩is entangled. (c) P03 displays broad SSR res- onances for J0/ω < 1 (|ϕ3⟩is nearly separable). Conversely for J0/ω > 1 ( |ϕ3⟩is entangled) resonances only appear for values of J0/ω satisfying the...

Figure 5. Comparison between numerical simulations (solid black lines) and analytical predictions obtained from the GVV theory (dashed red lines) for a pure SSR condition, ϵ0/ω = 1 and J0/ω = 2.5, in all the cases as a function of the driving amplitude A/ω. (a) Quasienergies ε↑↑and ε↓↓. (b) Effective coupling u and dynamic Stark shift difference δ↑↑−δ↓↓. Note that their respective zeros do not coincide. (c)Double-averaged transition probability P01. CDT (P01 = 0) occurs at the roots of u. The maximum population transfer(P01 = 0.5) is for the condition δ↑↑−δ↓↓= 0.
